title: Basics of HTML and CSS
section: Week 01 - Principles and Practices , Project management
content:
Basics of HTML and CSS#Disclaimer#All the steps in this guide are written for Windows users because I use Windows. Git works on other systems too, but some things may look different on Linux or Mac.
Choosing a Text Editor#Before starting with Git or making websites, we need a text editor. A text editor helps us to:
Write code (HTML, CSS, Markdown) Edit files Organize our project Some popular text editors on Windows are:
Visual Studio Code Atom Selecting a Text Editor#I chose Visual Studio Code because:
I already know how to use it It looks clean and easy It works well with Git It supports many programming languages You can use any editor you like.
Downloading Visual Studio Code#To download VS Code:
Open Google Chrome Go to the official Visual Studio Code website Download the Windows .exe installer Always download from the official website to stay safe.
Installing Visual Studio Code#During installation, most options can stay the same.
But make sure this option is checked:
✅ Add “Open with Code” action to Windows Explorer This helps open folders easily in VS Code by right-clicking.
After this, VS Code is ready to use.
Installing Helpful Extensions#Extensions make coding easier.
Live Server Extension#Live Server helps to:
Open your website in a browser Automatically refresh when you save files Steps:
Open VS Code Go to the Extensions (Ctrl + Shift + X) Search for Live Server Click Install Save Typing / Auto Save#To avoid losing work:
Click on File Select Auto Save This ensures files are saved automatically while typing.
Getting Started with HTML#HTML is used to create websites.
Creating a New Project in VS Code#Open VS Code Click on File → Open Folder Create a new folder and give it a name (for example: html-basics) Open it This folder will store your project files.
Creating a New HTML File#Create a new file Name it index.html Basic HTML Structure#<!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head> <title>Page Title</title> </head> <body> <h1>This is a Heading</h1> <p>This is a paragraph.</p> </body> </html>What This Code Means#<!DOCTYPE html>#tells the browser this is an HTML file
<html>#starts the webpage
<head>#contains page info
<title>#shows the name on the browser tab
<body>#contains what we see on the page#Common HTML Elements#Headings and Paragraphs#<h1> to <h6> are used for headings (largest to smallest) <p> is used for paragraphs Example:
<h1>Main Heading</h1> <p>This is a paragraph of text.</p> ## Images in HTML Images are added using the `<img>` tag: ```html <img src="image.jpg" alt="Sample Image"> src= image location alt= text if image doesn’t load Hyperlinks#Links are created using the <a> tag:
<a href="https://fabacademy.org">Fab Academy</a> href specifies the URL of the link destination Introduction to Basic CSS#CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is used to make websites look nice.
Example:
<style> body { background-color: lightgray; } h1 { color: blue; } </style>This changes the background color and heading color.
My Test Website#As part of this week, I made a small website to practice HTML.#🌐 Test Site:
https://nb6rules.github.io/fab-purely-html-site/ ⚠️ Note:
This website was created purely for testing and learning purposes. It will not be regularly updated and does not represent the final structure of my Fab Academy documentation.
My Main Fab Academy Website#The actual Fab Academy documentation website, which will be continuously updated and contain detailed weekly documentation, is hosted at:
🌐 Fab Academy Site:
https://fabacademy.org/2026/labs/kochi/students/nadec-biju/ Source Code Repository#All my website files are stored on GitHub.
📂 GitHub Repository:
https://github.com/NB6RULES/fab-purely-html-site/deployments/github-pages Reference#For a more detailed introduction to HTML and CSS, refer to:
https://www.w3schools.com/html/ https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML https://www.w3schools.com/css/

title: Git Tutorial
section: Week 01 - Principles and Practices , Project management
content:
A Git Tutorial (Windows)#Introduction#Git is a version control system used to track changes in files over time.
It allows users to:
Keep a history of changes Revert to older versions if something breaks Work systematically on projects In Fab Academy, Git is used to manage documentation, code, and design files.
Installing Git on Windows#Open a web browser Go to the official Git website:
https://git-scm.com/ Download the Windows installer Run the installer and keep the default options After installation, Git Bash will be available on your system.
What is Git Bash?#Git Bash is a terminal application that allows running Git commands on Windows.
It provides a Linux-like command line environment, which is commonly used in Fab Academy documentation.
To open Git Bash:
Right-click inside a folder Select “Open Git Bash here” Configuring Git#Before using Git, user details need to be configured:
git config --global user.name "Your Name" git config --global user.email "your-email@example.com"Verify the configuration:
git config --global --listNext Steps#Learn the basic Git commands Set up a GitHub or GitLab account if you haven’t already Connecting GitLab to Local Git Using SSH#As part of Fab Academy, GitLab is used to manage and submit documentation repositories.
This section explains how to log in to GitLab, clone the repository using SSH, and configure SSH keys on a Windows system.
Logging in to GitLab#Open a web browser Go to the Fab Academy GitLab instance Log in using the credentials provided by Fab Academy Navigate to your personal repository Copying the Repository Using SSH#Open your repository page on GitLab Click on the Clone button Select SSH Copy the SSH repository URL Generating an SSH Key (Windows)#Open Git Bash and run the following command:
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-email@example.com" Press Enter to accept the default file location Optionally set a passphrase, or press Enter to leave it empty This will generate:
A private key A public key Adding the SSH Key to the SSH Agent#Start the SSH agent:
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519Copy the public SSH key using:
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pubThen:
Select and copy the entire output Go to GitLab → Preferences → SSH Keys Paste the key into the Key field Click Add key Cloning the Repository#Navigate to the folder where you want to store the project and run:
git clone git@gitlab.com:username/repository-name.git Verifying the Connection#To verify that SSH is working correctly:
ssh -T git@gitlab.com Basic Daily Git Workflow#After the initial setup, the following workflow is used daily to update documentation and project files.
Check the current status of the repository:
git status Stage all modified files:
git add .Commit the changes with a meaningful message:
git commit -m "Week 01 documentation update"Push the changes to the remote repository:
git push

title: About Me
content:
About Me#Hello! I’m Nadec Biju, a Mechanical Engineering student at CUSAT (2022-2026) passionate about digital fabrication, robotics, and innovative product development.
I’ve always been drawn to the moment when an idea stops being just a thought and starts becoming something real.
For as long as I can remember, I’ve loved taking things apart, understanding how they work, and building them back better. Making, breaking, fixing, and redesigning is where I feel most comfortable. Over the years, this interest has grown into building real projects like off-road machines, hydrogen buggies, mechanical systems, and many CAD models that started as rough sketches and became working parts.
CAD is where my imagination gets shape. It is where messy ideas turn into clean designs, proper measurements, and mechanisms that actually work in the real world.
I enjoy solving problems that do not come with instructions, designing things that do not exist yet, and pushing projects from “what if” to “it works.” For me, engineering is not just about formulas or software. It is about creating things that move, improve, fail, and finally do something meaningful.
Skills#CAD & Design: Solidworks, Siemens NX, Fusion 360 | Fabrication: 3D Printing, Laser Cutting, CNC | Programming: Python, ESP32, Arduino
Certifications#🏆 SOLIDWORKS Additive Manufacturing Associate – Dassault Systèmes 🏆 SOLIDWORKS CAD Design Associate – Dassault Systèmes 🏆 SOLIDWORKS Electrical Design Associate – Dassault Systèmes 🏆 SOLIDWORKS Sustainability Associate – Dassault Systèmes Gallery#❮❯My Journey#Apr 2025 - PresentTeam Captain — Tarusa MotorsportsLeading 30+ member team designing Hydrogen Baja vehicles. Overseeing CAD, vehicle dynamics, and manufacturing.
Aug 2025 - PresentTrackside Intern — Indian Motorsports AcademyWorking with professional race engineers in Formula 4 and Indian Racing League championships.
Jan 2025 - PresentFounder — Layer6Founded CAD design and 3D printing consultancy for prototyping and product design services.
Jan 2025 - Aug 2025Product Development Intern — Pre6 TechFounding team intern at AI startup by Amod Malviya (Founder of Udaan, ex-CTO Flipkart).
Apr 2024 - Mar 2025Team Manager — Tarusa MotorsportsLed team to AIR 7 Overall, AIR 1 Cost Award, AIR 1 Statics, AIR 2 Simulation.
Mar 2024 - Apr 2025Vice Chairman — SAE CUSATLed student committee, managed flagship events: Inventiva, SAE INDIA Tier Events, and Cadable.
Sep 2023 - Feb 2024Design Lead — Hermes CUSATLed design for SAE Bicycle Challenge. Secured AIR 5 and 1st Place Best Design.
Dec 2023 - Feb 2025Resident Maker & Intern — TinkerHubManaged 3D printers, mentored students on hardware projects, conducted maker events.
Nov 2023 - Nov 2025Intern — CUSAT FablabDeveloped solid-state fan using piezoelectric crystals. Applied rapid prototyping techniques.
Jan 2023 - Mar 2024Powertrain Member — Tarusa MotorsportsWorked on powertrain for Hydrogen-BAJA. Team achieved AIR 1.
Contact#📞 +91 7558804529 | 📍 Kochi, Kerala | 📧 nadecbiju6@gmail.com | 💼 LinkedIn

title: Agreement
content:
Fab Academy Student Agreement#The Fab Academy is responsible for:#Teaching principles and practices of digital fabrication Arranging lectures, recitations, meetings, and events for the class Evaluating and providing feedback on student work Offering clear standards for completing assignments Certifying and archiving student progress Supervising class preparation Reviewing prospective students, instructors, and labs Providing central staff and infrastructure for students, instructors, and labs Fund-raising for costs not covered by student tuition Managing and reporting on the program’s finances, results, and impacts Publicizing the program Promoting a respectful environment free of harassment and discrimination Encouraging a diverse, accessible, and equitable community I am a Fab Academy student, responsible for:#Attending class lectures and participating in reviews Developing and documenting projects assigned to introduce and demonstrate skills, in enough detail to be able to reproduce them Honestly reporting on my work, and appropriately attributing the work of others (both human and machine, including AI prompts) Being prepared to answer questions about my work Allowing the Fab Academy to share my work (with attribution) in the class for purposes compatible with its mission Working safely Leaving workspaces in the same (or better) condition than I found them Participating in the upkeep of my lab Ensuring that my tuition for local and central class costs is covered Following locally applicable health and safety guidance Recognizing limits on lab and staff time Promoting a respectful environment free of harassment and discrimination Signed by committing this file in my repository:
Nadec Biju
View Student Agreement Document

title: Getting Started With Documentation
section: Week 01 - Principles and Practices , Project management
content:
How I Started Making My Documentation Website#So I made a basic HTML website first to understand how websites work. But then I wanted something better for my Fab Academy stuff.
I talked to my friend Rishi Krishna (https://rishikrishna.com/) and he told me about this cool thing called Hugo. It’s way easier than writing HTML for everything!
Why Hugo is Pretty Cool#Hugo takes simple text files and turns them into a whole website. It’s awesome because:
You just write in Markdown (which is super easy) It makes all the pages and menus for you You can add tons of pages without it getting messy The website loads really fast You don’t have to worry about the design stuff as much This is perfect for Fab Academy because there’s SO much stuff to document every week.
Getting Hugo on My Computer (Windows)#Step 1: Download It#Open your browser Go to https://gohugo.io/ Download the Windows (extended) version Unzip the file you downloaded Step 2: Make Windows Know About Hugo#Put the hugo.exe file somewhere (I used C:\Hugo\bin) Open System Properties → Environment Variables Add that folder to the Path thing Close and reopen VS Code or your terminal To check if it worked, open Git Bash or Command Prompt and type:
hugo versionMaking a New Hugo Website#Open Visual Studio Code and open the folder where you want your website.
Type this in the terminal:
hugo new site nadec-bijuThis makes a bunch of folders that look like this:
my-fab-docs/├── archetypes/├── content/├── layouts/├── static/├── themes/└── hugo.toml What All Those Folders Do#Folder/File What It’s For content/ This is where all your pages go (in Markdown) static/ Put your pictures and files here themes/ This controls how your website looks hugo.toml Settings for your whole website Using Hugo with VS Code#Open your Hugo folder in VS Code Get these helpful extensions: Markdown Preview Hugo Syntax Highlighting (if you want) To see your website while you work on it:
hugo serverNow you can see changes in your browser right away!
Writing Pages in Markdown#Hugo uses Markdown which is way easier than HTML.
To make a new page:
hugo new week-01/index.mdThis makes a new file in the content/ folder.
Basic Markdown Stuff#Making Headers## Big Header ## Medium Header ### Small HeaderWriting Paragraphs#Just type normally and it makes a paragraph.Making Lists#- Thing 1 - Thing 2 - Thing 3Adding Links#[Fab Academy](https://fabacademy.org)Adding Pictures#![Description of image](/images/sample.png) How I Set Up My Fab Academy Website#My documentation website is organized like this:
Each week gets its own page If I need more detail, I make sub-pages All my pictures go in the static/ folder I use Markdown for everything so it stays simple This makes it super easy to find stuff and add more as the course goes on.
Where to Put Different Stuff#What You’re Adding Where It Goes Weekly homework content/week-XX/ Pictures static/images/ Files to download static/files/ Design stuff themes/ Only Uploading the public Folder to Git#When Hugo makes your website, everything goes into the public/ folder. That’s the actual website that people see.
All the other folders (content, themes, etc.) are just for making the website, not for showing it.
For Fab Academy, we only need to upload the finished website files.
Why We Only Upload the public Folder#Fab Academy servers just need the HTML files Hugo makes the public folder fresh every time anyway It keeps things smaller and cleaner We don’t need all the extra files online Making a .gitignore File#This file tells Git what NOT to upload.
Step 1: Create It#Open your Hugo folder in VS Code Make a new file called: .gitignoreStep 2: Tell It What to Ignore#Put this in the file:
# Don't track Hugo source folders content/ themes/ layouts/ archetypes/ resources/ # Don't track config and temp files .hugo_build.lock node_modules/ # Ignore everything by default * # But DO track the public folder !public/ !public/**This means:
Ignore all the Hugo source files Only upload the public folder Making the public Folder#Before uploading, build your website:
hugoThis updates the public folder with all your latest changes.
Uploading to Git#After building:
git status git add public git commit -m "Update website" git pushNow only the public folder gets uploaded!
Important Thing to Remember#Hugo remakes the public folder every time you build.
So if you edit something directly in public, it’ll get deleted next time!
Always edit your Markdown files, never the public folder.
How to Add a New Page#Here’s what I do every time I want to add something new:
Step 1: Make the Page#hugo new docs/assignment/week-2/_index.mdStep 2: Edit the Top Part#Every page starts with some info at the top:
+++ title = "Week 2 - Computer-Aided Design" weight = 2 draft = false +++ Thing What It Does title The name that shows up weight Changes the order (smaller number = higher up) draft false means show it, true means hide it Step 3: Write Your Stuff#Under that, just write normally in Markdown:
# Week 2 - Computer-Aided Design ## What I Did This week I learned about CAD programs... ## Programs I Used - Fusion 360 - SolidWorksStep 4: Add Pictures#Put pictures in static/images/ Use them like this: ![My CAD thing](/images/week2-cad.png)Step 5: Check How It Looks#Run this to see your changes:
hugo serverGo to http://localhost:1313 in your browser.
Step 6: Build and Upload#When it looks good:
hugo git add public git commit -m "Add Week 2 stuff" git push When to Use Markdown vs HTML#Hugo lets you use both Markdown and HTML.
What You’re Doing Use This Example Normal text, headers, lists Markdown ## Header, - list Pictures Markdown ![pic](/images/pic.png) Links Markdown [text](url) Tables Markdown ` Cool custom stuff HTML + CSS <div class="custom"> Interactive things HTML + JavaScript <script>...</script> Easy rule: Markdown for writing, HTML for fancy stuff.
Making Cool Stuff: Picture Slider#For my About Me page, I made a picture slider to show my projects. Here’s how:
The HTML Part#<div class="carousel-container"> <div class="carousel"> <div class="carousel-slide fade"> <img src="images/gallery-1.jpg" alt="Project 1"> </div> <div class="carousel-slide fade"> <img src="images/gallery-2.jpg" alt="Project 2"> </div> <div class="carousel-slide fade"> <img src="images/gallery-3.jpg" alt="Project 3"> </div> </div> <a class="carousel-prev" onclick="changeSlide(-1)">❮</a> <a class="carousel-next" onclick="changeSlide(1)">❯</a> </div>Making It Look Good (CSS)#<style> .carousel-container { position: relative; max-width: 800px; margin: 40px auto; overflow: hidden; border-radius: 12px; box-shadow: 0 8px 25px rgba(0,0,0,0.15); } .carousel { position: relative; background: #f0f0f0; aspect-ratio: 16/9; } .carousel-slide { display: none; width: 100%; height: 100%; } .carousel-slide img { width: 100%; height: 100%; object-fit: cover; } .carousel-slide.active { display: block; animation: fadeIn 0.8s ease-in-out; } @keyframes fadeIn { from { opacity: 0; } to { opacity: 1; } } .carousel-prev, .carousel-next { cursor: pointer; position: absolute; top: 50%; width: auto; margin-top: -22px; padding: 16px; color: white; font-weight: bold; font-size: 18px; transition: 0.3s ease; border-radius: 3px; background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.5); user-select: none; z-index: 10; } .carousel-prev:hover, .carousel-next:hover { background-color: rgba(0,0,0,0.8); } .carousel-prev { left: 0; } .carousel-next { right: 0; } </style>Making It Work (JavaScript)#<script> let currentSlide = 0; let autoSlideInterval; function showSlide(n) { const slides = document.querySelectorAll('.carousel-slide'); if (n >= slides.length) { currentSlide = 0; } if (n < 0) { currentSlide = slides.length - 1; } slides.forEach(slide => slide.classList.remove('active')); if (slides.length > 0) { slides[currentSlide].classList.add('active'); } } function changeSlide(n) { currentSlide += n; showSlide(currentSlide); resetAutoSlide(); } function autoSlide() { currentSlide++; showSlide(currentSlide); } function resetAutoSlide() { clearInterval(autoSlideInterval); autoSlideInterval = setInterval(autoSlide, 4000); } document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() { showSlide(currentSlide); autoSlideInterval = setInterval(autoSlide, 4000); }); </script> Making Cool Stuff: Timeline#For my My Journey section, I made a timeline to show my experiences.
The HTML Part#<div class="timeline-container"> <div class="timeline-line"></div> <div class="timeline-item" data-year="2025"> <div class="timeline-dot"></div> <div class="timeline-content"> <span class="timeline-date">Apr 2025 - Present</span> <h3>Team Captain — Tarusa Motorsports</h3> <p>Leading 30+ member team designing Hydrogen Baja vehicles.</p> </div> </div> <div class="timeline-item" data-year="2024"> <div class="timeline-dot"></div> <div class="timeline-content"> <span class="timeline-date">Apr 2024 - Mar 2025</span> <h3>Team Manager — Tarusa Motorsports</h3> <p>Led team to AIR 7 Overall, AIR 1 Cost Award.</p> </div> </div> </div>Making It Look Cool (CSS)#<style> .timeline-container { position: relative; max-width: 800px; margin: 40px auto; padding: 20px 0; } .timeline-line { position: absolute; left: 20px; top: 0; bottom: 0; width: 4px; background: linear-gradient(to bottom, #007bff, #00d4ff); border-radius: 2px; } .timeline-item { position: relative; margin-left: 60px; margin-bottom: 30px; opacity: 0; transform: translateX(-30px); transition: all 0.6s ease-out; } .timeline-item.visible { opacity: 1; transform: translateX(0); } .timeline-dot { position: absolute; left: -48px; top: 5px; width: 16px; height: 16px; background: #007bff; border: 4px solid #fff; border-radius: 50%; box-shadow: 0 0 0 4px #007bff33; z-index: 1; } .timeline-item.visible .timeline-dot { animation: pulse 2s infinite; } @keyframes pulse { 0%, 100% { box-shadow: 0 0 0 4px #007bff33; } 50% { box-shadow: 0 0 0 8px #007bff22; } } .timeline-content { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f8f9fa 0%, #e9ecef 100%); padding: 20px; border-radius: 12px; box-shadow: 0 4px 15px rgba(0,0,0,0.1); border-left: 4px solid #007bff; transition: all 0.3s ease; } .timeline-content:hover { transform: translateY(-5px); box-shadow: 0 8px 25px rgba(0,0,0,0.15); } .timeline-date { display: inline-block; background: #007bff; color: white; padding: 4px 12px; border-radius: 20px; font-size: 0.8em; margin-bottom: 10px; } .timeline-content h3 { margin: 10px 0 8px 0; color: #333; font-size: 1.1em; } .timeline-content p { margin: 0; color: #666; font-size: 0.95em; line-height: 1.5; } </style>Making It Appear When You Scroll (JavaScript)#<script> (function() { var items = document.querySelectorAll('.timeline-item'); var observer = new IntersectionObserver(function(entries) { entries.forEach(function(entry) { if (entry.isIntersecting) { entry.target.classList.add('visible'); } }); }, { threshold: 0.2, rootMargin: '0px 0px -50px 0px' }); items.forEach(function(item) { observer.observe(item); }); })(); </script> What I Asked ChatGPT to Make These#Here’s what I told ChatGPT to get it to make the slider and timeline:
For the Picture Slider#Create a responsive image carousel/slider for a Hugo markdown page with: - Fade transition between slides - Previous/Next navigation arrows - Auto-slide every 4 seconds - Mobile responsive design - Dark mode support - Clean modern styling with rounded corners and shadow - Pure HTML, CSS, and JavaScript (no external libraries)For the Timeline#Create a vertical timeline component for a Hugo markdown page to display work experience with: - Animated appearance on scroll using Intersection Observer - Gradient line connecting timeline items - Pulsing dot animation for each entry - Date badge, title, and description for each item - Hover effects on timeline cards - Dark mode support - Mobile responsive layout - Pure HTML, CSS, and JavaScript (no external libraries)Tips for Using ChatGPT#Be specific about what you want it to look like Say “no external libraries” if you want simple HTML/CSS/JS Ask for dark mode if your theme has it Ask for mobile responsive so it works on phones Try again - start simple, then make it better Websites That Helped Me#These websites helped me figure out how to do all this:
Hugo – Static Site Generator
https://gohugo.io/
Hugo Documentation
https://gohugo.io/documentation/
Markdown Guide
https://www.markdownguide.org/
Fab Academy Documentation Archive
https://fabacademy.org/
Hugo Book Theme (GitHub)
https://github.com/alex-shpak/hugo-book
Rishi Krishna
https://rishikrishna.com/
These helped me understand how to make websites, use Markdown, and organize my Fab Academy documentation properly.

title: Planning Final Project
section: Week 01 - Principles and Practices , Project management
content:
Final Project – Initial Planning & Ideas#This page documents the initial brainstorming and planning phase for my Fab Academy final project.
At this stage, the focus is on exploring multiple ideas that integrate mechanical design, electronics, embedded systems, and digital fabrication.
Idea 1: TinkerWave – Kinetic Light Installation#TinkerWave is an interactive kinetic art installation.
It consists of approximately 30 individual tube lights, where the internal LEDs are removed and replaced with NeoPixel LED strips, allowing full control over color and animation.
The tubes are:
Suspended from the ceiling using twine Arranged parallel to each other with sufficient spacing Supported on both ends by twine connected to stepper-motor-driven pulleys By independently raising or lowering each side, the installation can form dynamic wave-like patterns, slopes, and motion-based visual effects.
Key Components#NeoPixel LEDs Stepper motors with pulley mechanisms Microcontroller for synchronized control Custom mechanical mounts and frames Idea 2: Filament Tracking Smart Dry Box#This project is a smart filament dry box designed to reduce filament runout and moisture-related print failures.
The system includes:
A sealed dry box enclosure Humidity sensing and active drying Real-time filament usage tracking Filament usage can be tracked by:
Counting extruder motor rotations or Measuring filament movement using a rotary encoder The remaining filament data is:
Updated periodically Written to an NFC tag attached to the filament spool Read using a phone or NFC reader before starting a print Key Components#Humidity sensor and drying system Rotary encoder / printer signal input NFC module Microcontroller Custom enclosure Idea 3: Modular Desktop Robotic Arm (Educational)#A compact, modular robotic arm designed for learning, experimentation, and prototyping.
The arm would:
Have multiple degrees of freedom Use 3D-printed structural components Use stepper motors or servo motors Be controlled using a custom-designed PCB Design focus areas include:
Modular joint architecture Clean cable routing Parametric CAD for easy scaling and modification Key Components#Motors (stepper / servo) Motor drivers Custom PCB Parametric CAD parts Idea 4: Smart Tool Cabinet with RFID-Based Access and Usage Tracking#A smart tool cabinet integrated into a work table, designed to manage and track tool usage.
Features include:
Individual drawers or compartments for tools RFID-based authentication to unlock drawers User identification to track who accessed a specific tool Tool presence detection using sensors Each access event can be logged to record:
Which tool was taken Who accessed it Time and duration of usage Key Components#RFID reader and RFID tags/cards Sensors for tool presence detection Motorized or solenoid-based drawer locks Microcontroller Custom table-integrated enclosure Idea 5: Fully 3D-Printable BLDC Motor with Custom FOC Controller#This project involves designing and building a fully 3D-printable BLDC motor intended for use as a robotic actuator, along with a custom-designed Field-Oriented Control (FOC) controller.
The motor would include:
3D-printed stator housing and rotor structure Embedded permanent magnets Custom winding configuration Mechanical design optimized for torque and smooth motion The electronics system would feature:
A custom BLDC motor driver PCB Phase current sensing Rotor position feedback Implementation of FOC algorithms Key Components#3D-printed motor components Copper windings and magnets Custom motor driver PCB Microcontroller capable of FOC Current and position sensing Next Steps#These ideas will be evaluated based on:
Feasibility within the Fab Academy timeline Complexity and level of system integration Learning depth across multiple domains Availability of components and fabrication methods One idea will be selected and developed into the final project.
Reflection#This planning phase helped clarify the type of project I want to pursue—one that tightly integrates mechanical systems, electronics, and embedded control, rather than isolated components.

title: Week 01 - Principles and Practices , Project management
content:
Week 01 - Principles and Practices , Project management#Week 01 marks the beginning of Fab Academy 2026. This week focuses on understanding the structure of the course and learning the fundamentals of project management, which involves planning, organizing, and tracking work effectively over time.
Rather than making physical artifacts, this week is about setting up a workflow that will be used consistently throughout the program.
What I Learned This Week#During this week, I explored the following topics:
Basics of HTML and CSS Understanding the fundamentals required to build and customize a personal documentation website.
Version control systems (Git and GitHub) Learning how to track changes, manage versions, and maintain a clean repository for documentation and files.
Getting Started With Documentation Understanding Fab Academy documentation standards and how to clearly communicate process, progress, and learning.
Planning Potential Final Projects Brainstorming and sketching ideas for a final project that aligns with my interests and skills.
Assignments#The assignments for this week included:
• Download, read, and sign the student agreement, and commit the file to my repository
• Completing a Git tutorial to understand version control basics
• Creating a personal website in the Fab Academy class archive
• Planning and sketching ideas for a potential final project
The planning of the week#Day Activity WED Review of the class with Neil THU Git + Website FRI About me SAT Sketch Final Project + Global open time SUN Documentation MON Recitation: version control TUE Regional Review This schedule helped balance learning new tools while keeping documentation up to date.
Tools Used#Software: Git GitHub VS Code HTML/CSS Reflection#This week helped me understand that Fab Academy is not just about completing assignments, but about building a disciplined workflow. Proper version control, structured documentation, and time management are essential skills that directly affect the quality of future work.
Establishing these habits early will make it easier to handle more complex fabrication, electronics, and programming tasks in the coming weeks.

title: Week 19 - Invention, Intellectual Property, And Income
content: Week 19 - Invention, Intellectual Property, And Income#
